Phonons: From Classical Lattice Dynamics to Quantum Transport ​

📅 Date: 2026-03-31 | 🏷️ Tags: Condensed Matter, Lattice Dynamics, Phonons | 📂 Category: Condensed Matter Notes

Introduction ​

This note provides a comprehensive treatment of phonons—the quantized excitations of crystal lattice vibrations. We develop the theory from classical Newtonian mechanics through canonical quantization, covering both monatomic (simple) and diatomic (compound) lattices, acoustic and optical phonon branches, polaritons, and van Hove singularities in the phonon density of states. Every derivation is presented in full detail.

Part I: Classical Lattice Dynamics ​

The Harmonic Approximation ​

Consider a crystal with atoms at equilibrium positions Rn0. Let un be the displacement of atom n from its equilibrium position. The potential energy V expanded to quadratic order in displacements gives the harmonic approximation:

V=V0+12∑n,n′∑α,βunαΦnn′αβun′β

where α,β∈{x,y,z} are Cartesian indices, and the force constant matrix is:

Φnn′αβ=∂2V∂unα∂un′β|u=0

Properties of Φ:

  1. Symmetry: Φnn′αβ=Φn′nβα (from equality of mixed partial derivatives)
  2. Translational invariance: Φnn′αβ=Φαβ(Rn0−Rn′0) depends only on relative equilibrium positions
  3. Zero sum rule: ∑n′Φnn′αβ=0 (uniform translation costs no energy)

The classical equations of motion follow from Newton's second law:

Mnu¨nα=−∂V∂unα=−∑n′,βΦnn′αβun′β≡Fnα

Bloch's Theorem and Plane Wave Solutions ​

Due to lattice periodicity, we seek normal mode solutions of the form:

un(t)=e(q)ei(q⋅Rn0−ωt)

where:

  • q is the wavevector in the first Brillouin zone
  • e(q) is the polarization vector (amplitude and direction of oscillation)
  • ω is the mode frequency (to be determined)

Substituting this ansatz into the equation of motion:

−Mω2eeiq⋅Rn0=−∑n′Φ(Rn0−Rn′0)eeiq⋅Rn′0

Let R=Rn0−Rn′0, then:

Mω2e=∑RΦ(R)ee−iq⋅R

This defines the dynamical matrix:

Dαβ(q)=1M∑RΦαβ(R)e−iq⋅R

The eigenvalue equation becomes:

∑βDαβ(q)eβ=ω2eα

This is a 3×3 Hermitian matrix eigenvalue problem for each q, giving three phonon branches (one longitudinal, two transverse) for a monatomic lattice.

Part II: Monatomic Lattice—Acoustic Phonons ​

The 1D Chain: Complete Derivation ​

Consider a 1D monatomic chain with:

  • Lattice constant a (equilibrium spacing)
  • Atom mass M
  • Nearest-neighbor spring constant K

Step 1: Set up the equation of motion

The potential energy depends on relative displacements:

V=12K∑n(un+1−un)2

The force on atom n:

Fn=−∂V∂un=−K[(un−un−1)−(un+1−un)]=K(un+1+un−1−2un)

Newton's equation:

Mu¨n=K(un+1+un−1−2un)

Step 2: Insert plane wave ansatz

un(t)=Aei(qna−ωt)

Note: Physical displacement is Re[un(t)].

Computing derivatives:

  • u¨n=−ω2un
  • un+1=Aei(q(n+1)a−ωt)=uneiqa
  • un−1=une−iqa

Substituting:

−Mω2un=K(eiqa+e−iqa−2)un

Step 3: Solve for dispersion relation

Cancel un≠0:

Mω2=K(2−eiqa−e−iqa)=2K(1−cos⁡(qa))

Using the identity 1−cos⁡θ=2sin2⁡(θ/2):

Mω2=4Ksin2⁡(qa/2)

Taking the positive root (frequency is positive):

ω(q)=2KM|sin⁡qa2|≡ωm|sin⁡qa2|

where ωm=2K/M is the maximum frequency at the zone boundary.

Properties of the Acoustic Dispersion ​

1. Long wavelength limit (|q|a≪1):

Expand sin⁡(x)≈x for small x:

ω(q)≈ωm|q|a2=2KM|q|a2=KMa|q|

This is linear dispersion ω=vs|q| with sound velocity:

vs=aKM

Physical interpretation: At long wavelengths, the discrete lattice appears as a continuous elastic medium, and phonons become ordinary sound waves.

2. Group velocity:

vg(q)=dωdq=vscos⁡(qa/2)⋅sgn(q)
  • At q=0: vg=vs (maximum, wave packet travels at sound speed)
  • At q=±π/a: vg=0 (standing wave at zone boundary)

3. Phase velocity:

vp(q)=ωq=vs|sin⁡(qa/2)||qa/2|

Dispersion is strongest near the zone boundary where vp≠vg.

4. Symmetry:

ω(q)=ω(−q)(time-reversal symmetry)ω(q+2π/a)=ω(q)(periodicity in reciprocal space)

Part III: Diatomic Lattice—Acoustic and Optical Phonons ​

Model Setup ​

Consider a 1D diatomic chain with:

  • Two atoms per primitive cell: masses M1 and M2 (assume M1<M2 without loss of generality)
  • Lattice constant a (primitive cell length)
  • Atoms arranged as: ⋯−M1−M2−M1−M2−⋯
  • Positions: M1 at 2na, M2 at (2n+1)a for integer n
  • Nearest-neighbor spring constant K (same for all bonds)

Let un = displacement of M1 at position 2na Let vn = displacement of M2 at position (2n+1)a

Equations of Motion ​

For M1 at position 2na:

The atom is connected to M2 at (2n−1)a (left) and (2n+1)a (right).

Spring forces:

  • Left spring: K[vn−1−un] (force to right if vn−1>un)
  • Right spring: K[vn−un]
M1u¨n=K(vn−1−un)+K(vn−un)=K(vn−1+vn−2un)

For M2 at position (2n+1)a:

Connected to M1 at 2na (left) and 2(n+1)a (right).

M2v¨n=K(un−vn)+K(un+1−vn)=K(un+un+1−2vn)

Bloch Ansatz ​

Seek plane wave solutions with wavevector q in the reduced zone [−π/2a,π/2a]:

un=Aei(2qna−ωt)vn=Bei(q(2n+1)a−ωt)=Beiqaei(2qna−ωt)

Note: The phase factor eiqa accounts for the position offset between sublattices.

Deriving the Matrix Equation ​

Step 1: Substitute into the M1 equation

−M1ω2Aei2qna=K[Beiq(2n−1)a+Beiq(2n+1)a−2Aei2qna]

Factor out ei2qna:

−M1ω2A=K[Be−iqa+Beiqa−2A]−M1ω2A=2K[Bcos⁡(qa)−A]

Step 2: Substitute into the M2 equation

−M2ω2Beiq(2n+1)a=K[Aei2qna+Aei2(n+1)qa−2Beiq(2n+1)a]

Factor out eiq(2n+1)a:

−M2ω2B=K[Ae−iqa+Aeiqa−2B]−M2ω2B=2K[Acos⁡(qa)−B]

Step 3: Matrix form

Rearranging both equations:

(2K−M1ω2)A−2Kcos⁡(qa)B=0−2Kcos⁡(qa)A+(2K−M2ω2)B=0

In matrix form:

(2K−M1ω2−2Kcos⁡(qa)−2Kcos⁡(qa)2K−M2ω2)(AB)=0

Solving the Eigenvalue Problem ​

For nontrivial solutions, the determinant must vanish:

(2K−M1ω2)(2K−M2ω2)−4K2cos2⁡(qa)=0

Expand:

4K2−2K(M1+M2)ω2+M1M2ω4−4K2cos2⁡(qa)=0

Using 1−cos2⁡(qa)=sin2⁡(qa):

M1M2ω4−2K(M1+M2)ω2+4K2sin2⁡(qa)=0

Let Ω=ω2. This is a quadratic equation:

M1M2Ω2−2K(M1+M2)Ω+4K2sin2⁡(qa)=0

Using the quadratic formula Ω=−b±b2−4ac2a:

Ω=2K(M1+M2)±4K2(M1+M2)2−16K2M1M2sin2⁡(qa)2M1M2Ω=K(M1+M2)M1M2±KM1M2(M1+M2)2−4M1M2sin2⁡(qa)

The two solutions are:

ω±2(q)=Kμ±K1μ2−4sin2⁡(qa)M1M2

where μ=M1M2M1+M2 is the reduced mass.

Explicit Form at Special Points ​

At q=0:

ω±2(0)=K(M1+M2)M1M2±K(M1+M2)M1M2
  • Acoustic: ω−2(0)=0 → ω−(0)=0
  • Optical: ω+2(0)=2K(M1+M2)M1M2=2K(1M1+1M2)

Long wavelength acoustic branch:

For small q, expand sin2⁡(qa)≈(qa)2:

ω−2≈Kμ−Kμ1−4μ2(qa)2M1M2

Using 1−x≈1−x/2 for small x:

ω−2≈Kμ−Kμ(1−2μ2(qa)2M1M2)=2Kμ(qa)2M1M2

With μ=M1M2/(M1+M2):

ω−2=2K(qa)2M1+M2ω−(q)≈|q|a2KM1+M2=vs|q|

Sound velocity: vs=a2K/(M1+M2)

At zone boundary q=π/2a:

sin2⁡(qa)=sin2⁡(π/2)=1ω±2=K(M1+M2)M1M2±K|M1−M2|M1M2
  • Acoustic: ω−2=2KM2 (assuming M2>M1)
  • Optical: ω+2=2KM1

Frequency gap at zone boundary:

Δω=2K(1M1−1M2)

Eigenvectors and Physical Interpretation ​

From the matrix equation, the amplitude ratio is:

BA=2K−M1ω22Kcos⁡(qa)

Acoustic mode (ω=ω−):

At q=0: ω−=0, so:

BA=2K2K=1

Both atoms move in phase with equal amplitude: A=B. This is a uniform translation.

Optical mode (ω=ω+):

At q=0: ω+2=2K(1/M1+1/M2)

2K−M1ω+2=2K−2K(1+M1/M2)=−2KM1M2BA=−2KM1/M22K=−M1M2

Atoms move out of phase with amplitude ratio M1A+M2B=0, meaning the center of mass remains fixed.

For ionic crystals (M1 positive ion, M2 negative ion), this oscillating dipole couples to electromagnetic radiation (infrared absorption).

Part IV: Canonical Quantization ​

Classical Normal Mode Coordinates ​

Step 1: Define Fourier-transformed coordinates

For the 1D monatomic chain with periodic boundary conditions (N atoms, length L=Na):

un=1N∑quqeiqna,uq=1N∑nune−iqna

Allowed wavevectors: q=2πmNa for m=0,±1,…,±(N/2−1),N/2

Step 2: Transform the Lagrangian

The classical Lagrangian:

L=∑nM2u˙n2−K2∑n(un+1−un)2

Kinetic energy:

∑nu˙n2=1N∑n∑q,q′u˙qu˙q′ei(q+q′)na=∑q|u˙q|2

(using ∑nei(q+q′)na=Nδq,−q′ and u−q=uq∗ for real un)

Potential energy:

∑n(un+1−un)2=∑q|uq|2⋅2(1−cos⁡(qa))=∑q|uq|2Mωq2K

Thus:

L=∑q(M2|u˙q|2−Mωq22|uq|2)

This is a sum of independent harmonic oscillators, one for each q.

Quantization Procedure ​

Step 1: Define canonical momenta

pq=∂L∂u˙q∗=Mu˙q

(Note: uq and uq∗ are treated as independent variables in the Lagrangian formalism for complex coordinates.)

Step 2: Impose commutation relations

[u^q,p^q′†]=iℏδqq′[u^q,u^q′]=[p^q,p^q′]=0

Step 3: Define creation and annihilation operators

For each mode q:

a^q=Mωq2ℏ(u^q+ip^qMωq)a^q†=Mωq2ℏ(u^q†−ip^q†Mωq)

Note: For standing wave boundary conditions, u^q†=u^−q and p^q†=p^−q.

Step 4: Verify commutation relations

[a^q,a^q′†]=Mωq2ℏ[u^q+ip^qMωq,u^q′†−ip^q′†Mωq′]=Mωq2ℏ([u^q,u^q′†]−iMωq′[u^q,p^q′†]+iMωq[p^q,u^q′†]+1M2ωqωq′[p^q,p^q′†])

Using [u^q,p^q′†]=iℏδqq′ and other commutators vanishing:

[a^q,a^q′†]=Mωq2ℏ(−i(iℏ)δqq′Mωq+i(−iℏ)δqq′Mωq)=Mωq2ℏ⋅2ℏMωqδqq′=δqq′

Also: [a^q,a^q′]=[a^q†,a^q′†]=0

Step 5: Express Hamiltonian

H^=∑qℏωq(a^q†a^q+12)

Phonon Number States ​

The eigenstates are labeled by occupation numbers |nq1,nq2,…⟩:

a^q†a^q|{nq}⟩=nq|{nq}⟩
  • nq=0,1,2,… (bosonic occupation)
  • a^q† creates a phonon in mode q
  • a^q annihilates a phonon in mode q

Thermal average (Bose-Einstein distribution):

At temperature T:

⟨nq⟩=1eβℏωq−1

Part V: Three-Dimensional Crystals ​

The Dynamical Matrix in 3D ​

For a crystal with s atoms per unit cell at positions rκ (κ=1,…,s), the dynamical matrix is a 3s×3s matrix:

Dκα,κ′β(q)=1MκMκ′∑RΦκα,κ′β(R)e−iq⋅R

where Φκα,κ′β(R) is the force constant between atom κ in the unit cell at origin and atom κ′ in the unit cell at R.

Diagonalization:

∑κ′βDκα,κ′β(q)eκ′β(λ)(q)=ωλ2(q)eκα(λ)(q)

There are 3s eigenvalues ωλ(q) labeled by λ=1,…,3s.

Classification of Phonon Branches ​

Acoustic branches (3 modes):

At q=0: ω(q=0)=0 for all 3 acoustic branches

  • One longitudinal acoustic (LA): atomic displacements ∥q
  • Two transverse acoustic (TA): atomic displacements ⊥q

Polarization vectors at q=0:

eκα(ac)(0)=1sδα,direction

All atoms in the unit cell move in the same direction (uniform translation).

Optical branches (3s−3 modes):

At q=0: ω(0)≠0 (finite frequency)

  • Atoms within each unit cell move relative to each other
  • Center of mass of each unit cell remains fixed
  • For ionic crystals, can couple to electromagnetic fields

Part VI: Phonon Density of States and van Hove Singularities ​

Definition of Density of States ​

The phonon density of states g(ω) counts the number of modes per unit frequency:

g(ω)=∑λ=13s∫BZd3q(2π)3δ(ω−ωλ(q))

Normalization:

∫0∞g(ω)dω=3s

(total number of phonon modes per unit cell).

Deriving the van Hove Singularity ​

Near a critical point qc where ∇qω|qc=0, expand:

ω(q)=ωc+12∑i=13αi(qi−qc,i)2

where αi are the principal curvatures (eigenvalues of the Hessian matrix).

Case 1: Minimum or Maximum (all αi>0 or all <0)

For a 3D minimum (all αi>0):

g(ω)∝∫d3qδ(ω−ωc−12∑iαiqi2)

Let ξi=αi/2qi, then:

g(ω)∝1α1α2α3∫d3ξδ(ω−ωc−ξ2)

In spherical coordinates:

∫d3ξδ(Δω−ξ2)=4π∫0∞ξ2dξδ(Δω−ξ2)=2πΔω

where Δω=ω−ωc>0.

g(ω)∝ω−ωc(for ω>ωc)

This is a square-root singularity at the band edge.

Case 2: Saddle Point (mixed signs)

For example, α1,α2>0 and α3<0:

g(ω)∝∫dξ1dξ2dξ3δ(Δω−ξ12−ξ22+|α3′|ξ32)

This leads to logarithmic singularities or discontinuities depending on dimensionality.

Physical origin: Vanishing group velocity vg=∇qω=0 at critical points causes modes to "pile up," enhancing g(ω).

Examples ​

1D Monatomic Chain:

ω(q)=ωm|sin⁡(qa/2)|

Near q=π/a (zone boundary), let q=π/a−δq:

ω≈ωmcos⁡(δq⋅a/2)≈ωm(1−(δq)2a28)g(ω)=Lπdqdω∝1ωm−ω

This diverges as (ωm−ω)−1/2 at the zone boundary.

Diatomic Chain:

The frequency gap between acoustic and optical branches at q=π/2a:

ωgap=2K/M1−2K/M2

No phonon states exist in this gap: g(ω)=0 for ω in the gap.

Part VII: Polaritons—Coupled Phonon-Photon Modes ​

Infrared-Active Optical Phonons ​

In ionic crystals, optical phonons at q=0 carry an oscillating dipole moment. For a diatomic crystal with effective ionic charge e∗:

Dipole moment per unit cell:

P=ne∗(u1−u2)

where n is the number density of unit cells.

This polarization couples to the macroscopic electric field E via:

Hint=−P⋅E

Dielectric Function ​

The dielectric response of the coupled phonon-photon system is described by:

ε(ω)=ε∞+Ωp2ωTO2−ω2−iγω

where:

  • ε∞: electronic (high-frequency) dielectric constant
  • ωTO: transverse optical phonon frequency at q=0
  • Ωp=ne∗2/με0: ionic plasma frequency
  • γ: phonon damping rate

Lyddane-Sachs-Teller (LST) Relation ​

The longitudinal optical frequency ωLO satisfies ε(ωLO)=0:

0=ε∞+Ωp2ωTO2−ωLO2

Solving for ωLO:

ωLO2=ωTO2+Ωp2ε∞

The static dielectric constant is:

ε0=ε(0)=ε∞+Ωp2ωTO2

Eliminating Ωp2:

ωLO2ωTO2=ε0ε∞

This is the LST relation. Since ε0>ε∞ for ionic crystals, ωLO>ωTO.

Polariton Dispersion ​

The coupled modes satisfy the dispersion relation:

ω2c2ε(ω)=q2

This gives two branches separated by a frequency gap (the Reststrahlen band) between ωTO and ωLO where ε(ω)<0 and no propagating modes exist.

Summary ​

ConceptMathematical ResultPhysical Interpretation
Monatomic dispersionω=2K/M|sin⁡(qa/2)|Acoustic phonons, ω∝q at small q
Diatomic dispersionTwo branches with gapAcoustic (in-phase) and optical (out-of-phase)
Sound velocityvs=aK/M or a2K/(M1+M2)Long-wavelength limit
Quantization[a^q,a^q′†]=δqq′Bosonic quasiparticles
van Hove singularityg(ω)∝ω−ωcCritical points where vg=0
LST relationωLO2/ωTO2=ε0/ε∞Screening of LO mode by ions

References ​

  1. Kittel, C. Introduction to Solid State Physics, 8th ed. (Wiley, 2005). Chapter 4

  2. Ashcroft, N.W. & Mermin, N.D. Solid State Physics (Brooks Cole, 1976). Chapters 22-23

  3. Li, Z. (李正中). Solid State Theory (《固体理论》), 2nd ed. (Higher Education Press, 2002). Chapter 2

  4. Cardona, M. & Güntherodt, G. (eds.) Light Scattering in Solids (Springer, 1982)

  5. Srivastava, G.P. The Physics of Phonons (Taylor & Francis, 1990)
